class_schedule.topic: Educational Psychology: Science of Learning and Teaching
learning_objectives: Define educational psychology and its role in improving instruction
Describe how cognitive development theories inform classroom practice
Explain the relationship between educational research and evidence-based teaching

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational psychology applies psychological science to optimize learning and instruction. It bridges laboratory research on cognition, motivation, and development with real-world classroom practice.
key_concepts: Evidence-based teaching practices derived from psychological research
Piaget and Vygotsky in the classroom: discovery learning vs. guided instruction
Zone of proximal development and scaffolding in educational contexts
Individual differences: intelligence, learning disabilities, giftedness
Action research and teacher-as-researcher model
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a lesson plan identifying which educational psychology principles are applied and which are violated

review.value: Temporary instructional support adjusted to the learner's current level; gradually removed as competence increases
Instructional practices supported by rigorous research; contrasts with tradition-based or intuition-based teaching

learning_objectives: Compare intrinsic and extrinsic motivation and their effects on learning
Explain self-determination theory and its three basic needs
Describe expectancy-value theory and its implications for student engagement

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation determines whether students engage, persist, and achieve. Understanding motivational theories allows educators to design environments that foster genuine engagement.
key_concepts: Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
Intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation: the overjustification effect
Expectancy-value theory: success expectation x subjective task value
Attribution theory (Weiner): locus, stability, controllability
Growth mindset (Dweck) and its application in educational settings
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign a reward system that undermines intrinsic motivation using SDT principles

review.value: External rewards for intrinsically interesting activities can undermine intrinsic motivation; controlling rewards are worse than informational rewards
Deci & Ryan: intrinsic motivation thrives when three basic psychological needs are met: autonomy, competence, and relatedness

class_schedule.topic: Cognitive Science of Learning: Memory and Transfer
learning_objectives: Apply encoding strategies to improve classroom learning
Explain transfer of learning and conditions that promote it
Evaluate common study strategies in light of cognitive science research
readings: Ormrod, Ch. 6-8; Brown, Roediger, & McDaniel, Make It Stick (selections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Decades of cognitive research have identified evidence-based strategies for durable learning. Many popular study strategies are ineffective, while less intuitive approaches produce stronger results.
key_concepts: Retrieval practice (testing effect) as a learning strategy
Spaced practice vs. massed practice
Interleaving vs. blocking for skill learning
Elaborative interrogation and self-explanation
Transfer: near vs. far; conditions that promote it (deep understanding, varied examples)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students experience interleaved vs. blocked practice with math problem types and compare performance

review.value: Self-testing is one of the most effective study strategies; produces better retention than rereading even with no feedback
Conditions that make learning harder in the short term (spacing, interleaving, generation) produce stronger long-term retention

learning_objectives: Explain social-emotional learning (SEL) competencies and their academic benefits
Discuss the relationship between belonging, engagement, and achievement

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective instruction requires a well-managed classroom. Research shows that proactive, relationship-based management outperforms reactive, punitive approaches.
key_concepts: Proactive management: clear expectations, routines, positive reinforcement
Functional behavior assessment: identifying the function of disruptive behavior
CASEL SEL framework: self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship skills, responsible decision-making
Belongingness and its impact on academic motivation and achievement
Restorative practices vs. zero-tolerance policies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Functional behavior assessment exercise: analyze a case of disruptive behavior and develop an intervention plan

review.value: Identifies the antecedents and consequences maintaining problem behavior; intervention targets the function (attention, escape, access) not just the behavior
Students who feel they belong in school show higher motivation, engagement, and achievement; belonging is a fundamental psychological need (Baumeister & Leary)

learning_objectives: Distinguish between formative and summative assessment
Explain the principles of valid and reliable classroom assessment
Discuss standards-based grading and alternative assessment approaches

lecture_notes.introduction: Assessment drives learning. How we measure student understanding profoundly shapes what and how students study. Effective assessment is aligned, fair, and informative.
key_concepts: Formative assessment: ongoing feedback to guide instruction
Summative assessment: evaluating achievement at the end of a unit
Backward design (Wiggins & McTighe): start with desired outcomes, then design assessment, then plan instruction
Rubric design: analytic vs. holistic rubrics
Test bias, fairness, and accommodations for diverse learners
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a rubric for an authentic assessment task using backward design principles

review.value: Start with learning goals, then design assessments that measure those goals, then plan instruction aligned to both
Assessment FOR learning: provides teachers and students with feedback during instruction to adjust teaching and studying strategies

learning_objectives: Describe Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles
Explain differentiated instruction for diverse learners
Discuss the inclusion of students with disabilities and English language learners

lecture_notes.introduction: Every classroom contains diverse learners. Effective teachers design instruction that provides multiple pathways to engagement, understanding, and expression.
key_concepts: Universal Design for Learning: multiple means of engagement, representation, and action/expression
Differentiation: adjusting content, process, product, and learning environment
Response to Intervention (RTI): tiered support model
English language learner strategies: comprehensible input, sheltered instruction
Twice-exceptional students: giftedness with learning disabilities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign a traditional lesson using UDL principles to increase accessibility

review.value: Proactively design curriculum with flexibility for diverse learners: multiple means of engagement (why), representation (what), and action/expression (how)
Three-tiered model: Tier 1 (high-quality instruction for all), Tier 2 (targeted small-group intervention), Tier 3 (intensive individualized intervention)

quizzes.topic: Motivation and Cognitive Science of Learning

questions.question: A teacher gives gold stars to first-graders for every book they read. Initially, reading increases, but after the star program ends, students read less than before the program started. Explain this phenomenon using self-determination theory.
questions.answer: This is the overjustification effect. Self-determination theory explains it through the concept of autonomy: the gold stars shifted the perceived locus of causality from internal (reading for pleasure) to external (reading for rewards). When the external reward was removed, the intrinsic motivation had been undermined, so reading dropped below baseline. SDT would recommend using rewards that are informational rather than controlling, and focusing on supporting autonomy (offering reading choices), competence (providing appropriately challenging books), and relatedness (sharing reading experiences with peers).

questions.question: Explain why interleaved practice is superior to blocked practice for learning mathematical problem types, even though students report feeling like they learn less during interleaving.
questions.answer: Interleaved practice (mixing different problem types within a session) is superior because it forces students to discriminate between problem types and select the appropriate strategy, which is the skill actually needed on tests. Blocked practice (doing all problems of one type, then switching) allows students to mindlessly apply the same strategy without discriminating. Students feel like they learn less during interleaving because the difficulty is higher and errors are more frequent, creating a metacognitive illusion. Processing fluency during blocked practice is mistaken for learning. Research by Rohrer and Taylor shows interleaving produces 43% better test performance despite lower practice performance.

rubric.key: Explains why interleaving produces better discrimination
Explains the metacognitive illusion (fluency vs. learning)

questions.question: A student repeatedly calls out in class without raising their hand. Using a functional behavior assessment perspective, identify two possible functions of this behavior and describe an intervention for each.
questions.answer: Function 1: Attention-seeking. The student calls out because it reliably produces teacher and peer attention. Intervention: planned ignoring of call-outs combined with differential reinforcement -- provide attention when the student raises their hand (DRA). Function 2: Escape from task difficulty. The student calls out to create a distraction when work is too challenging. Intervention: adjust task difficulty to the student's level, provide pre-task check-ins, and teach an appropriate way to request help (replacement behavior). The key insight is that identical behaviors can serve different functions, so interventions must target the function, not just the topography.

rubric.key: Notes that function must drive intervention

questions.question: Describe the three principles of Universal Design for Learning and give a specific classroom example for each.
questions.answer: 1) Multiple means of engagement (the WHY of learning): Offer student choice in topics for a research project, increasing autonomy and interest. 2) Multiple means of representation (the WHAT of learning): Present new vocabulary using text, images, audio pronunciation, and physical demonstrations, so students can access content through their strongest modality. 3) Multiple means of action and expression (the HOW of learning): Allow students to demonstrate understanding of a historical event through a written essay, oral presentation, digital multimedia project, or graphic novel, so assessment does not confound content knowledge with a single mode of expression.

rubric.key: Correctly names and defines all three principles
Provides specific, appropriate classroom examples for each
Demonstrates understanding of proactive inclusive design

major_assignments.title: Evidence-Based Instructional Unit Design
major_assignments.description: Design a 5-lesson instructional unit for a specific grade level and subject area. Ground every design decision in educational psychology research. Include backward design framework, differentiation strategies, formative and summative assessments, and a classroom management plan.
objectives: Apply backward design to create an aligned instructional unit
Incorporate evidence-based learning strategies from cognitive science
Design differentiated instruction for diverse learners using UDL
Create valid and reliable assessments aligned to learning objectives
deliverables: Complete 5-lesson instructional unit with daily plans
Assessment rubrics for formative and summative assessments
Reflection paper (1,500 words) citing minimum 8 peer-reviewed sources

criteria: Clear learning objectives aligned to standards
Incorporates retrieval practice, spacing, or interleaving
UDL principles applied across all lessons
Rigorous backward design with evidence-based strategies
Well-aligned assessment system with clear rubrics

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of educational psychology concepts and applications

classroom_observations.description: Structured observations of teaching and learning in real classrooms

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering motivation, cognition, and learning theory applications

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Classroom observation hours are mandatory and cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Instructional unit plans will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Lesson planning may be discussed with peers. All submitted work must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All instructional materials must be original or properly attributed. Copying lesson plans from online sources is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to learning management systems, instructional design tools, and university library databases.
